query: conditions where direct Coombs test is negative

retrieved_docs.title: Coombs Test -- Interfering Factors
retrieved_docs.content: When performed correctly and utilized in the appropriate clinical context, direct antiglobulin testing has been shown to demonstrate a positive predictive value of 97% to 99%, although a more recent study involving hospitalized patients reported a false positive rate of up to 7% to 8% in patients without any evidence of hemolysis clinically or histologically. The majority of these false positives show a low grade of agglutination, though up to 1% of these results may demonstrate higher grades of agglutination. [12] In another study consisting of a cohort of healthy, non-hospitalized individuals, the incidence of a positive DAT result without evidence of hemolysis was found to be 0.1%; approximately two-thirds of this cohort expressed IgG positivity. [13] Several other confounding variables can affect the accuracy of DAT and IAT test results:

retrieved_docs.title: Coombs Test -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Antiglobulin testing, particularly qualitative direct antiglobulin testing, is clinically useful in cases where there is clinical suspicion of autoantibody-induced RBC hemolysis. DAT testing typically involves using a polyspecific reagent consisting of IgG and complement C3. Indirect antiglobulin testing is clinically useful for the detection of circulating antibodies that have the potential to induce RBC hemolysis; this test is most commonly utilized for RBC phenotyping and in crossmatch screening for blood transfusion. A positive antiglobulin result requires analysis in the clinical context to make an accurate diagnosis. Healthcare costs and the burden of laboratory time can be minimized by screening with a polyspecific reagent before confirming the antibody with monospecific or quantitative analysis. Rarely, autoimmune hemolysis may be suspected even without positive DAT testing; in this instance, quantitative DAT testing may help identify less common antibody subtypes other than IgG or C3. In the absence of other confounding variables (see “ Interfering Factors ” above), positive antiglobulin testing indicates the presence of hemolysis by antibodies directed against native RBCs. There are several major areas of clinical significance:

retrieved_docs.title: Coombs Test -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Alloimmune-mediated hemolytic transfusion reaction (AHTR): AHTR occurs when a post-transfusion specimen develops a newly found alloantibody. The formation of an alloantibody can occur as quickly as within 2 to 3 days. [22] The development of alloantibodies results in a positive DAT test but may or may not be associated with hemolysis. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Diagnostic Tests -- Indirect Antiglobulin Test or Indirect Coombs Test
retrieved_docs.content: Furthermore, the indirect Coombs test aids in determining the specificity of the antibodies, which helps in guiding management decisions, including selecting appropriate blood products for transfusion and assessing the need for immunosuppressive therapy. Additionally, serial indirect Coombs testing can track changes in antibody titers over time, providing valuable insights into treatment response and disease progression in patients with AIHA.

retrieved_docs.title: Coombs Test -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: ABO blood group typing: In blood transfusions and hematopoietic stem cell transplants, indirect antiglobulin testing can be used to identify the RBC phenotype to minimize the chances of donor incompatibility.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Diagnostic Tests -- Direct Antiglobulin Test or Direct Coombs Test
retrieved_docs.content: After incubation, the RBCs are rewashed to remove the unbound reagent, and the suspension is examined microscopically for signs of agglutination or antibody binding. A positive result, indicated by agglutination, suggests antibodies or complement proteins bound to the RBC surface, confirming the diagnosis of immune-mediated hemolysis. [40] The direct Coombs test may be performed in different variations, including tube and gel methods, but the basic principle remains the same. The severity of hemolysis is generally correlated with the strength of DAT positivity.

retrieved_docs.title: Coombs Test -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Coombs test is necessary when autoimmunity to red blood cells is a consideration in the differential diagnosis, including warm and cold hemolytic anemia. Following are some indications where antiglobulin testing becomes useful: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia Drug-induced immune hemolytic anemia Alloantibodies-mediated hemolytic transfusion reactions Hemolytic disease of the newborn Systemic lupus erythematosus (without hemolytic anemia)

retrieved_docs.title: Coombs Test -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Antiglobulin testing can be either direct antiglobulin testing (DAT) or indirect antiglobulin testing (IAT). The principle of DAT is to detect the presence of antibodies attached directly to the RBCs, which takes place by washing a collected blood sample in saline to isolate the patient’s RBCs; this procedure removes unbound antibodies that may otherwise confound the result. IAT, by contrast, is used to detect unbound antibodies to RBCs, which may be present in the patient’s serum. Direct antiglobulin testing adds a monospecific or polyspecific reagent to the washed RBCs to detect bound IgG and/or complement C3. In practice, many laboratories first use the polyspecific reagent that can detect both IgG and C3; a positive result is followed by monospecific testing to characterize the antibody further. [1] For indirect antiglobulin testing, serum from a blood sample gets isolated, and native RBCs are removed. The isolated serum sample then gets incubated with foreign RBCs of known antigenicity. Antiglobulin reagent is then added, and the presence of agglutination indicates a positive result.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Diagnostic Tests -- Indirect Antiglobulin Test or Indirect Coombs Test
retrieved_docs.content: The indirect antiglobulin tests assess antibodies in the patient's serum that may react with donor RBCs. This test is essential for identifying alloantibodies and ensuring compatibility for blood transfusions. [51] This test is crucial for ensuring the compatibility of donor blood with the recipient's serum to prevent adverse reactions during blood transfusions and minimize the risk of hemolytic disease in the newborn. The procedure involves collecting a blood sample from the patient, isolating the serum containing antibodies, and incubating it with donor RBCs of known antigenic characteristics.

retrieved_docs.title: Coombs Test -- Potential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The potential diagnosis of the Coombs test includes pre-transfusion testing, hemolytic transfusion reaction, and autoimmune or drug-induced hemolytic anemias. [6] [7] There are several causes of a positive Coombs test, such as: Hemolytic transfusion reactions Autoantibodies to intrinsic RBC antigens Hemolytic disease of the newborn Drug-induced antibodies Passively acquired alloantibodies, such as from donor plasma or immunoglobulin Nonspecifically adsorbed proteins Complement activation because of bacterial infection, alloantibodies, or autoantibodies Antibodies produced by passenger lymphocytes

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Diagnostic Tests -- Direct Antiglobulin Test or Direct Coombs Test
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with hemolytic anemia lacking recent transfusions or cold agglutinin or Donath-Landsteiner antibodies, a positive DAT for IgG and/or C3d is typically indicative of warm AIHA. Cold agglutinin disease is mainly characterized by DAT positivity for C3d (usually negative or weakly positive for IgG), indicating the involvement of IgM antibodies. For clinical significance, cold agglutinin titers are typically more than 512; however, levels above 64 are considered positive. Mixed AIHA manifests as DAT positivity for both IgG and high-titer cold agglutinins. Additionally, atypical forms of AIHA may occur, presenting as DAT-negative cases driven by IgA or warm IgM antibodies. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Anemia Screening -- Diagnostic Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Direct antiglobulin test: This assessment, also known as the Coombs test, detects immune-mediated hemolytic anemia by identifying antibodies bound to RBCs.

retrieved_docs.title: Coombs Test -- Normal and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with autoimmune hemolytic anemia, the degree of agglutination typically correlates with the severity of hemolysis. If no macroscopic agglutination appears, the sample is examined microscopically to ensure no aggregates. A sample exposed to a reagent demonstrating aggregates of at least 3 to 5 cells under microscopic examination is considered a positive result. Agglutination typically takes 5 to 10 minutes after adding the reagent. Direct antiglobulin testing may also be measured quantitatively using enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), flow cytometry, or other immunoassay techniques. [8] [9] [10] [11] Quantitative sample measurement may be necessary when isolation of a specific antibody is desired, such as in cases of autoimmune hemolysis due to antibodies other than IgG or C3.

retrieved_docs.title: Coombs Test -- Interfering Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Antiphospholipid syndrome - cross-reactivity between antiphospholipid antibodies and RBC membranes can result in falsely positive DAT testing, as reported by Win et al [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Coombs Test -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA): AIHA is traditionally the most recognized cause of positive antiglobulin testing, and has been the topic of extensive study. The classification “AIHA” serves as an overarching descriptor that unifies a large group of diagnoses with differing etiologies that cause hemolysis using antibodies against RBCs. [21] The classification can be dichotomized further by considering factors such as warm versus cold agglutination and primary versus secondary cause. AIHA may also be drug-induced or syndromic (see “Evans syndrome”). Further characterization of diseases that fall under this classification is beyond the scope of this entry.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Immunity disorders are classified as a subset of نقص غلوبيولين الدم, which refers to a deficiency of blood globulins. This globulin deficiency results in reduced levels of immunoglobulins, compromising the body’s immune defenses and predisposing individuals to infections and other immune‑related complications. Recognizing immunity disorders within the broader context of نقص غلوبيولين الدم helps clinicians understand the underlying immunological deficit and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at restoring adequate immune function.

retrieved_docs.content: disorders are placed under the broader category of blood‑globulin deficiency (نقص غلوبيولين الدم), indicating that globulin deficiency underlies or is related to various immune system abnormalities. Recognizing malaise, fatigue, and the link between immunity disorders and globulin deficiency, together with the shared pharmacologic strategies and contraindications, supports an integrated diagnostic and therapeutic approach in patient care.

The autoimmune disorder category also includes crescentic glomerulonephritis, linking autoimmunity to severe glomerular disease. Collectively, these interconnections form an integrated framework that links renal disease, dialysis therapies, hematologic abnormalities, vascular complications, immune‑mediated disorders, and related surgical and diagnostic procedures. This framework supports comprehensive patient assessment, standardized documentation, and coordinated multidisciplinary care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia, non‑regenerative anemia, arthritis, hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions. This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2694
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 70, a high‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in patients with الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease). Because Dextran 70 can increase blood viscosity and promote coagulation, its use in individuals who already have a propensity for clot formation may exacerbate the underlying thrombotic condition and increase the risk of serious vascular complications. Clinicians should therefore avoid administering Dextran 70 to patients diagnosed with الاعتلال الخثري and consider alternative volume‑expanding therapies that do not carry this pro‑thrombotic risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 202

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2038
retrieved_docs.content: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية) is clinically associated with hypocalcemia (نقص كالسيوم الدم), indicating that patients with this immune‑mediated destruction of red blood cells may also exhibit low blood calcium levels. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as it suggests that calcium monitoring and appropriate management of hypocalcemia should be considered when evaluating and treating individuals with autoimmune hemolytic anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2852
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 40, a low‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in the presence of الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease). Because Dextran 40 can increase blood viscosity and promote clot formation, its use in patients with thrombotic disorders may exacerbate the underlying condition and raise the risk of further vascular occlusion. Consequently, clinicians should avoid administering Dextran 40 to individuals diagnosed with الاعتلال الخثري and consider alternative volume‑expanding therapies that do not carry this risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1751
retrieved_docs.content: Factor VIII may be used to treat coagulation and hemorrhagic disorders, as it helps correct deficiencies in the clotting cascade that underlie these bleeding conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2737
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 75, a high‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in patients with الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease); using dextran 75 in this condition may promote clot formation and worsen clinical outcomes, so clinicians should avoid prescribing it to individuals who have thrombotic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2276
retrieved_docs.content: The category of autoimmune disorders (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية) encompasses several distinct conditions, including التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد (cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). Each of these diseases is characterized by an inappropriate immune response that targets the body’s own tissues, leading to joint inflammation, destruction of red blood cells, or inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing that these diverse clinical entities share a common autoimmune basis aids clinicians in diagnosing, monitoring, and applying immunomodulatory therapies across this spectrum of immune‑mediated illnesses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2177
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل), arthritis (التهاب المفاصل), acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب), autoimmune‑mediated hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية), and coronary arteritis (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are all classified as members of the autoimmune disorder group (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية SMQ). These conditions share a common pathophysiology in which the immune system mistakenly attacks self‑tissues, leading to hematologic abnormalities in the various anemias and inflammatory damage in joints and coronary arteries. Recognizing this shared autoimmune basis guides clinicians toward similar diagnostic approaches—such as autoantibody testing—and therapeutic strategies, including immunosuppressive or immunomodulatory treatments, while emphasizing the need to monitor for systemic involvement across both blood‑forming and vascular systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: Failure (biologic function) is associated with الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ, indicating that a general loss of biological function can be linked to hepatorenal failure—a severe condition in which both liver and kidney functions are compromised. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as it highlights the need to assess and manage systemic organ failure comprehensively in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1950
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 70 and Dextran 75 are plasma‑expanding agents that can help prevent venous thrombosis (التخثر الوريدي). However, both Dextran 70 and Dextran 75 are contraindicated in patients who have thrombotic disease (الاعتلال الخثري). This dual relationship underscores the importance of evaluating a patient’s underlying thrombotic condition before administering either Dextran 70 or Dextran 75, as their antithrombotic benefit in the venous system must be balanced against the risk of worsening an existing thrombotic disorder.
